hero.tagline: Maine's small claims court offers a simplified process for claims up to $10,000, with mandatory mediation before trial.

at_a_glance.label: Most you can sue for
at_a_glance.detail: Same cap for individuals and businesses

at_a_glance.label: Appeal window
at_a_glance.detail: Plaintiff appeals on law; defendant on law or fact

limits.split_claims_explanation: If your claim exceeds $10,000, you may choose to file in small claims court and waive the excess amount, or file in regular District or Superior Court to seek the full amount. Splitting one dispute into multiple claims to stay within the small claims limit is not permitted.

claim_categories.title: Contracts and money owed
claim_categories.blurb: Maine allows you to file claims for breaches of contract and money owed up to $10,000. The statute of limitations varies depending on the type of contract.

claims.name: Breach of a written contract
claims.example: A contractor signed a $4,000 contract to remodel your kitchen, took the deposit, and never finished.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the signed contract, payment proof, and any correspondence showing the breach.

claims.name: Breach of an oral contract
claims.example: You agreed verbally to lend a friend $2,000, and they failed to repay it.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Gather any evidence of the agreement, such as emails or witness statements.

claims.name: Unpaid personal loan
claims.example: You loaned $5,000 to a colleague with the understanding it would be repaid in a year, but they haven't paid you back.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide documentation of the loan agreement and any repayment attempts.

claims.name: Default on a promissory note
claims.example: A borrower signed a promissory note to repay $8,000 over two years but stopped making payments after six months.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Present the original promissory note and records of payments made.

claims.name: Unpaid invoice for services rendered
claims.example: You provided $3,500 worth of consulting services to a client who has not paid the invoice.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Submit the invoice, service agreement, and any communication regarding payment.

claims.name: Unpaid balance on an open account
claims.example: A customer has an outstanding balance of $2,000 on their account with your business and refuses to pay.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide account statements and records of transactions.

claims.name: Unpaid rent by tenant
claims.example: Your tenant owes $1,500 in unpaid rent and has vacated the property without paying.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Present the lease agreement and records of unpaid rent.

claims.name: Payment with a bad check
claims.example: A customer paid for goods with a $500 check that was returned for insufficient funds.
claims.notes: Provide the bounced check, bank notice, and any communication with the issuer.
claims.damage_boost: Check amount + service charge up to $40 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

claims.name: Co-signer seeking repayment
claims.example: You co-signed a $7,000 loan for a friend who defaulted, and now you're seeking repayment from them.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide the loan agreement and proof of payments you've made.

claim_categories.title: Consumer disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Maine's small claims court handles various consumer disputes up to $10,000, including issues with products and services.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective product
claims.example: You bought a $1,200 appliance that stopped working after a week, and the seller refuses to refund or replace it.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from purchase. Bring the purchase receipt, warranty, and any communication with the seller.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages or $25, whichever is greater; possible attorney's fees.

claims.name: Unsatisfactory service
claims.example: A cleaning service charged you $500 but did not perform the agreed-upon tasks.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from service date. Provide the service agreement and evidence of inadequate service.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages or $25, whichever is greater; possible attorney's fees.

claims.name: Faulty auto repair
claims.example: A mechanic charged you $2,000 for repairs that did not fix the issue, and refuses to refund.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from repair date. Bring repair invoices and any expert opinions on the faulty repair.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages or $25, whichever is greater; possible attorney's fees.

claims.name: Substandard contractor work
claims.example: A contractor was paid $9,000 for home renovations but performed shoddy work that needs redoing.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from completion. Provide the contract, payment records, and photos of the work.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages or $25, whichever is greater; possible attorney's fees.

claims.name: Vendor failed to deliver services
claims.example: You paid a DJ $1,000 for an event, but they never showed up and refuse to refund.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from event date. Bring the service agreement and proof of payment.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages or $25, whichever is greater; possible attorney's fees.

claims.name: Unauthorized subscription charges
claims.example: A company continues to charge your credit card $50 monthly despite your cancellation requests.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from first unauthorized charge. Provide cancellation requests and billing statements.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages or $25, whichever is greater; possible attorney's fees.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective used car
claims.example: You bought a used car for $7,500 that broke down within a week, and the dealer refuses to address the issue.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from purchase. Bring the sales contract, warranty, and repair records.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages or $25, whichever is greater; possible attorney's fees.

claims.name: False advertising leading to financial loss
claims.example: A company advertised a product as 'free,' but you were charged $100 upon delivery.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from purchase. Provide the advertisement and proof of charges.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages or $25, whichever is greater; possible attorney's fees.

claim_categories.title: Property damage
claim_categories.blurb: Maine's small claims court addresses property damage claims up to $10,000, including vehicle collisions and rental property damage.

claims.name: Damage from a vehicle collision
claims.example: Another driver caused $4,000 in damage to your car and refuses to pay for repairs.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from accident date. Provide police reports, repair estimates, and photos.

claims.name: Neighbor damaged your property
claims.example: A neighbor's tree fell onto your fence, causing $2,500 in damage, and they refuse to compensate.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from damage date. Provide photos and any communication with the neighbor.

claims.name: Tenant caused damage to rental property
claims.example: A former tenant left $3,000 worth of damage to your rental unit beyond normal wear and tear.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from discovery. Provide the lease agreement, photos, and repair invoices.

claims.name: Damage caused by someone else's pet
claims.example: A neighbor's dog entered your yard and destroyed $1,200 worth of landscaping.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from incident. Provide photos and any communication with the pet owner.

claims.name: Damage to property left in someone's care
claims.example: You left a $2,000 bicycle with a friend who returned it damaged, refusing to compensate.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from return. Provide evidence of the property's condition before and after.

claim_categories.title: Landlord-tenant disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Maine's small claims court handles monetary disputes between landlords and tenants up to $10,000, excluding eviction proceedings.

claims.name: Failure to return security deposit
claims.example: Your landlord withheld your $1,500 security deposit without justification after you moved out.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from lease termination. Provide the lease agreement and move-out inspection report.

claims.name: Failure to provide itemized list for deposit deductions
claims.example: Your landlord deducted $800 from your deposit without providing an itemized list of damages.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from lease termination. Provide the lease agreement and any communication regarding the deposit.

claims.name: Landlord failed to maintain habitable conditions
claims.example: Your apartment had no heat for two weeks in winter, and the landlord did not address the issue.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from incident. Provide photos, repair requests, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord unlawfully retained tenant's property
claims.example: After eviction, your landlord kept your furniture worth $3,000 and refuses to return it.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from incident. Provide evidence of ownership and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord conducted an illegal eviction
claims.example: Your landlord changed the locks without notice, preventing you from accessing your apartment.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from incident. Provide the lease agreement and any communication regarding the eviction.

claims.name: Failure to return prepaid rent after lease termination
claims.example: You paid first and last month's rent, but the landlord did not return the last month's rent after you moved out early.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from lease termination. Provide the lease agreement and proof of payment.

claims.name: Tenant failed to pay rent
claims.example: Your tenant owes $2,000 in unpaid rent and has vacated the property without paying.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide the lease agreement and records of unpaid rent.

claims.name: Tenant repaired and deducted from rent
claims.example: You paid $500 to fix a leaking roof after the landlord failed to address it, and deducted the cost from your rent.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from repair. Provide repair receipts and any communication with the landlord.

claim_categories.title: Employment and wage disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Maine's small claims court addresses employment-related disputes up to $10,000, including unpaid wages and final paychecks.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay wages
claims.example: Your employer owes you $3,000 in unpaid wages for work performed over the past two months.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from due date. Provide pay stubs and any communication regarding payment.
claims.damage_boost: Unpaid wages plus interest; possible attorney's fees.

claims.name: Employer withheld final paycheck
claims.example: After resigning, your employer refused to issue your final paycheck of $1,500.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from due date. Provide resignation letter and any communication regarding the final paycheck.
claims.damage_boost: Unpaid wages plus interest; possible attorney's fees.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned commissions
claims.example: You earned $4,000 in sales commissions that your employer refuses to pay.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from due date. Provide commission agreements and sales records.
claims.damage_boost: Unpaid wages plus interest; possible attorney's fees.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay overtime wages
claims.example: You worked 20 hours of overtime but were only paid your regular hourly rate.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from due date. Provide timesheets and pay stubs.
claims.damage_boost: Unpaid wages plus interest; possible attorney's fees.

claim_categories.title: Personal claims
claim_categories.blurb: Maine's small claims court handles personal disputes up to $10,000, including minor injuries and property issues.

claims.name: Minor personal injury
claims.example: You slipped on a neighbor's icy walkway, resulting in $2,000 in medical bills.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from injury. Provide medical records and any communication with the property owner.

claims.name: Failure to return loaned property
claims.example: You loaned a friend your $1,000 camera, and they refuse to return it.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from demand for return. Provide evidence of the loan and any communication regarding its return.

claims.name: Theft of personal property
claims.example: A roommate took your $500 watch without permission and refuses to return it.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from theft. Provide evidence of ownership and any communication with the individual.

claim_categories.title: Vehicle-specific disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Maine's small claims court addresses vehicle-related disputes up to $10,000, including mechanic issues and wrongful towing.

claims.name: Dispute over mechanic's repair work
claims.example: A mechanic charged you $3,000 for repairs that did not fix the issue, and refuses to refund.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from repair date. Bring repair invoices and any expert opinions on the faulty repair.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages or $25, whichever is greater; possible attorney's fees.

claims.name: Vehicle was wrongfully towed
claims.example: Your car was towed from a legal parking spot, resulting in $500 in fees.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from towing date. Provide towing receipts and photos of the parking area.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages or $25, whichever is greater; possible attorney's fees.

exclusions.explanation: Small claims court does not handle eviction proceedings; these must be filed in regular District Court.
Cases involving disputes over the ownership of real estate are excluded from small claims court.
Claims involving defamation, such as libel or slander, are not suitable for small claims court due to their complexity.
Claims alleging professional malpractice require expert testimony and are beyond the scope of small claims court.
Issues such as divorce, child custody, and support are handled by Family Court, not small claims court.
Disputes involving wills, estates, and trusts are under the jurisdiction of Probate Court.
Small claims court does not have jurisdiction over claims against federal agencies or the U.S. government.
Class action lawsuits are complex and exceed the procedural scope of small claims court.
Small claims court cannot issue orders requiring a party to do or refrain from doing something.
Disputes over workers' compensation benefits are handled by the Workers' Compensation Board.

damages.punitive_explanation: Punitive damages are not awarded in small claims court; however, statutory penalties may apply in certain cases, such as bad check claims.

statutory_multipliers.explanation: Check amount + service charge up to $40 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

damages.attorney_fees_explanation: Attorney's fees may be awarded in cases involving violations of the Unfair Trade Practices Act or bad check claims.

filing_options: District Court in the county where the defendant resides
District Court in the county where the events giving rise to the claim occurred

venue_rules.scenario: Where the defendant's registered agent is located
filing_options: District Court in the county where the defendant's registered agent is located

where_to_file.consequences_of_wrong_venue: Filing in the wrong venue may result in dismissal or transfer of the case, potentially causing delays and additional costs.
where_to_file.e_filing_notes: eFiling is available in certain District Courts; check the Maine Judicial Branch website for current availability.

forms.name: Application to Proceed Without Payment of Fees

eligibility_bullets: Receiving need-based public assistance (SNAP, TANF, SSI)
Household income below 125% federal poverty line
waiver.coverage_notes: The waiver covers filing fees and may cover service fees; check with the court clerk.
waiver.if_you_win_notes: If you win, the waived fees may be added to the judgment against the defendant.

fees.fees_recoverable_from_loser_notes: If you win, filing fees and reasonable service costs are added to the judgment against the defendant.

statute_of_limitations.discovery_rule_notes: The discovery rule applies to fraud claims, starting the statute of limitations when the fraud is discovered.
statute_of_limitations.tolling_notes: The statute of limitations may be tolled if the defendant is out of state, the plaintiff is a minor, or in cases of bankruptcy.

pre_filing.demand_letter_notes: While not required, sending a demand letter can facilitate settlement and demonstrate good faith.
pre_filing.bad_check_notice_rule: For bad check claims, a 30-day written notice demanding payment is required before filing suit.
pre_filing.wage_claim_demand_rule: For unpaid wage claims, a written demand is recommended to document the employer's refusal to pay.
pre_filing.landlord_tenant_pre_filing_notes: Landlords must provide a forwarding address for security deposit returns; tenants should document repair requests before deducting from rent.
pre_filing.consumer_protection_notes: Under the Unfair Trade Practices Act, a demand letter is recommended to allow the business to address the issue before litigation.

forms.name: Statement of Claim
forms.description: Initiates a small claims case by outlining the plaintiff's allegations and requested relief.

forms.name: Application to Proceed Without Payment of Fees
forms.description: Requests a waiver of court fees due to financial hardship.

forms.name: Disclosure Subpoena
forms.description: Orders a judgment debtor to appear in court to disclose assets for judgment collection.

forms.name: Motion to Set Aside Default Judgment
forms.description: Requests the court to vacate a default judgment due to specific reasons.

forms.name: Notice of Appeal
forms.description: Notifies the court and opposing party of the intent to appeal a small claims judgment.

methods.name: Sheriff personal service
methods.description: Sheriff or deputy personally delivers the documents to the defendant.

methods.name: Certified mail
methods.description: Clerk sends documents via certified mail with return receipt requested.

methods.name: Private process server
methods.description: Authorized individual personally delivers documents to the defendant.

service.business_service_rule: Serve the registered agent or an officer of the corporation; for sole proprietors, serve the individual directly.
service.out_of_state_defendant_notes: Service must comply with Maine rules and the laws of the state where the defendant is located.
service.cant_find_defendant_notes: If the defendant cannot be located, the court may allow service by publication upon request.
service.avoiding_service_notes: If the defendant is evading service, document attempts and request alternative service methods from the court.

response_and_default.response_notes: Defendants are not required to file a written answer but should appear at the hearing to present their defense.
response_and_default.default_process: If the defendant fails to appear at the hearing, the court may enter a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.
response_and_default.prove_up_notes: Even if the defendant defaults, the plaintiff must present evidence to support the claim before a judgment is entered.

motion_to_vacate_form.name: Motion to Set Aside Default Judgment

hearing.lawyers_allowed_notes: Parties may represent themselves or hire an attorney; corporations may be represented by an employee or principal.
hearing.format: Bench trial with informal procedures; parties present evidence and testimony directly to the judge.
what_to_bring: Names and contact info for witnesses
hearing.interpreter_request_process: Request an interpreter from the court clerk as soon as possible before the hearing date.
hearing.decision_timing: On the spot or mailed within a few weeks
hearing.on_site_mediation_notes: Mediation is offered before the hearing to attempt settlement; if unsuccessful, the case proceeds to trial.

appeals.type_notes: Defendants can appeal on questions of law or fact; plaintiffs can appeal on questions of law only.
appeals.default_judgment_notes: Defendants must first file a motion to set aside the default judgment; if denied, they may appeal that decision.

collection.post_judgment_interest_notes: Interest accrues on unpaid judgments at the statutory rate until paid in full.

methods.name: Wage garnishment
methods.blurb: Have a portion of the debtor's wages withheld until the judgment is paid.
methods.description: File a motion for a disclosure hearing; if the debtor has income, request a wage garnishment order from the court; serve the order on the employer, who will withhold and remit payments.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Most effective when debtor is W-2 employed at a stable job.

methods.name: Bank levy
methods.blurb: Seize funds from the debtor's bank account to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution from the court; serve it on the debtor's bank; the bank freezes the account and remits funds to the court.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor has sufficient funds in the account.
exemptions: $1,000 in a bank account is exempt from levy

methods.name: Property lien
methods.blurb: Place a lien on the debtor's real property to secure the judgment amount.
methods.description: Record the judgment with the county registry of deeds; the lien must be satisfied before the property can be sold or refinanced.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns real property and intends to sell or refinance.

methods.name: Writ of execution
methods.blurb: Authorize the sheriff to seize and sell the debtor's non-exempt property.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution from the court; the sheriff seizes and sells the debtor's property; proceeds are applied to the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns valuable non-exempt property.

collection.judgment_renewal_process: File a motion to renew the judgment before it expires; court may grant renewal for an additional period.
collection.bankruptcy_effect_notes: Filing for bankruptcy imposes an automatic stay on collection efforts; certain judgments may be discharged in bankruptcy.
collection.priority_notes: Judgment liens are prioritized by the date of recording; earlier liens have higher priority.

businesses.self_representation_notes: A corporation or other legal entity can be represented by an employee or other principal even if not a lawyer.
businesses.fictitious_name_notes: Businesses must use their legal entity name when filing or defending a claim.
businesses.sole_proprietor_employee_exception: A sole proprietor may represent themselves or be represented by an employee.
businesses.licensing_notes: Unlicensed contractors may face challenges in enforcing contracts if a license is required for the work performed.

pitfalls.title: Filing in the wrong venue
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing in the incorrect District Court may result in dismissal or transfer, causing delays and additional costs.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Ensure you file in the correct venue based on where the defendant resides, where the events occurred, or where the defendant's registered agent is located.

pitfalls.title: Exceeding the monetary limit
pitfalls.what_happens: If your claim exceeds $10,000 and you file in small claims court, you must waive the excess amount or risk dismissal.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Consider filing in regular District or Superior Court if your claim exceeds the small claims limit.

pitfalls.title: Ignoring service requirements
pitfalls.what_happens: Improper service can lead to delays or dismissal of your case.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Follow Maine's rules for serving the defendant, using authorized methods and filing proof of service with the court.

pitfalls.title: Missing the statute of limitations
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing after the statute of limitations has expired will result in dismissal of your claim.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Be aware of the specific statute of limitations for your type of claim and file within the required timeframe.

pitfalls.title: Failing to appear at the hearing
pitfalls.what_happens: If you do not appear, the court may dismiss your case or enter a default judgment against you.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Attend all scheduled hearings or notify the court in advance if you cannot attend.

pitfalls.title: Not collecting the judgment
pitfalls.what_happens: Winning a judgment does not guarantee payment; you must take steps to collect.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Familiarize yourself with collection methods such as wage garnishment, bank levies, and property liens.

recent_changes.title: Increase in small claims jurisdictional limit
recent_changes.description: The monetary limit for small claims cases increased from $6,000 to $10,000, allowing for higher-value disputes to be resolved in small claims court.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer?
faqs.answer: No, you are not required to have a lawyer in small claims court. The process is designed to be accessible for individuals representing themselves. However, you may choose to hire an attorney if you prefer.

faqs.question: How long does it take?
faqs.answer: The timeline varies, but most small claims cases are resolved within 30 to 90 days from filing to judgment, depending on court schedules and whether mediation is successful.

faqs.question: What's the maximum I can sue for?
faqs.answer: As of January 1, 2026, the maximum amount you can sue for in Maine's small claims court is $10,000, excluding interest and costs.

faqs.question: What happens if the defendant doesn't show up?
faqs.answer: If the defendant fails to appear at the hearing, the court may enter a default judgment in your favor. You will still need to present evidence to support your claim before the judgment is granted.

faqs.question: Can I appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes, both parties have the right to appeal a small claims judgment. Defendants can appeal on questions of law or fact, while plaintiffs can appeal on questions of law only. The appeal must be filed within 30 days of the judgment.

faqs.question: How long is a judgment good for?
faqs.answer: In Maine, a small claims judgment is valid for 20 years. If the judgment is not satisfied within that period, you may need to take additional legal action to enforce it.

state_specific_notes: Maine's small claims court offers mandatory mediation before proceeding to trial, aiming to resolve disputes amicably.
As of January 1, 2026, the jurisdictional limit for small claims cases increased to $10,000.
eFiling is available in certain District Courts; check the Maine Judicial Branch website for current availability.
Corporations and other legal entities can be represented by an employee or principal, even if they are not attorneys.
Small claims court cannot issue orders for specific performance or injunctions; it is limited to monetary judgments.

interesting_findings: Maine's small claims court provides free mediation services to help parties resolve disputes without a trial.
The increase in the jurisdictional limit to $10,000 allows for a broader range of disputes to be handled in small claims court.
eFiling initiatives are expanding access to the courts and streamlining the filing process for self-represented litigants.
